topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery opening was a blur of polite chatter and forced smiles. I stood near my latest piece, a vibrant abstract canvas that had taken weeks of painstaking effort to complete. Then I saw it. On the lower edge of the painting, subtly rendered in stark black ink, was a depiction of my own signature motif—a swirling vortex I’d designed myself. It wasn't just a likeness; it was a deliberate, undeniable mark, placed by someone else. A cold knot tightened in my chest, stealing the air from my lungs. I forced a laugh, a brittle sound that felt alien in my own ears. When the artist approached, their eyes flicked toward the tattoo, a fleeting, knowing glance that made my skin prickle with a sudden, sharp awareness of being seen, judged, and fundamentally undermined. I quickly pivoted, my hands gripping a glass of champagne so tightly my knuckles were white, and retreated toward the quiet corner of the room, desperate to disappear into the shadows.

[story 2]

I traced the line of my newest sculpture, a delicate arrangement of reclaimed metal. It was meant to be a statement about fragility, an exploration of broken beauty. But as I stepped back to admire it, my gaze snagged on the base. Etched into the metal, with a precision that spoke of deep familiarity, was a stylized representation of my unique symbol. My breath hitched. A wave of intense awkwardness washed over me, making my palms sweat. I felt my posture stiffen, every muscle tense beneath my linen shirt. When the curator complimented the piece, their voice seemed too loud, too intrusive. I managed a tight nod, my gaze fixed rigidly on the floor, acutely aware of the way every eye in the room seemed to be cataloging the discrepancy—my creation, marked by another hand. I mumbled a vague thank you, the sound barely audible, and began to pack my portfolio with hurried, jerky movements.

[story 3]

It happened during the private viewing. I was examining a large oil painting, one I considered my magnum opus. As I leaned in closer, the light caught a detail I hadn't noticed before: a faint, almost hidden line running beneath the texture of the brushstrokes. It was a tattoo. My own private design, the one I’d been agonizing over for months, was incorporated into the artwork, but it wasn't painted; it was etched into the canvas itself, a permanent signature from an unknown source. A profound sense of exposure settled over me, heavy and suffocating. My mouth went dry. I felt a sudden, desperate need to look away, to physically turn my body away from the object that now felt tainted, compromised. When the owner of the piece finally addressed me, their tone was cordial, but the way their eyes lingered on the marked area sent a tremor through me, leaving me feeling utterly exposed under the scrutiny of the entire room.
